sentences.text: It's really Tot-kohm-losh, but I guess you wouldn't have known.
My parents-- my father was a watchmaker in the-- in the village.
He was actually the village watchmaker.
And my mother was the lady who kept house.
And my father was a very religious person.
And we went to the synagogue every Friday.
He went to the synagogue every morning.
But he had one weakness.
He loved cowboy movies.
So occasionally we went to see cowboy movies.
So I remember the cowboy movies we saw were-- what was that guy's name?
Zorro or something.
You know, the guy with the whip, you know, for the Z's?
And, and my mother was helping out in the store.
Did you have siblings?
No.
I'm a--
I'm an only child, yeah.
I was born by myself, I don't know-- I really don't know where.
I may have been born in a-- in a hospital outside Totkomlos.
But I'm not sure exactly where it was.
Well, very early in our career, you know, we realized that it wasn't too safe to walk alone on the streets because the other boys used to beat up on us, OK, before we got to school.
You know, in the school, nobody bothered us, really, you know.
But on the street while we were going to the school, you know, the kids were really bothering us.
We went to a Hungarian public school where you were supposed to-- you couldn't wear your religious articles.
In other words, I couldn't wear my hat.
And I had to dress like anybody else, so it was strange.
And it was-- it felt a little bit hostile, you know, like in a hostile environment.
But nevertheless, we were taken there because it was important for our education.
It was a small village, so we didn't have any-- we didn't have any Jewish schools.
Some of the largest towns, you know, they had Jewish schools with the-- with the religious part of the school and the secular part of the school.
But in our village, we couldn't afford it, so we had to go separately to public school, school, and then we went separately to cheder.
No, majority of them were gentiles.
As a matter of fact, a lot of people wouldn't allow-- here's my stuff.
Just a minute.
I lost a little bit.
I'll put it back myself.
OK.
A lot of the-- a lot of the families who were really, really ultra-orthodox, they wouldn't let their kids go to school.
They taught their kids themselves, you know, the secular things, reading, arithmetic, and so on.
And then later on, they sent them to bigger cities, OK?
But that's how it was?
Did you associate with the children from the school after school hours?
No.
I don't remember ever associating-- no, there was one.
No, but we never associated outside school, never did.
No, not that I remember.
I don't-- I don't remember.
The teachers were all nice to us.
We were small.
We were six years old, as I remember, or five years old, so, you know, when you're that young, you know, you wouldn't pick up those things, you know?
Yeah.
Yes.
After cheder, usually the parents picked us up or we walked home in groups.
We went home.
And it was usually around after the evening prayer, so that was-- by the time we got home it was 8:30, 9:00.
Wel, it was not safe, you know, to walk home alone, of course, sometimes.
Well, you don't know.
I mean, even here, you know, you're afraid to walk home alone.
So we walked in groups.
And then we were worried that somebody-- maybe some of the kids would beat up on us.
So that's all there was to it.
So we were worried that we were being beaten up or anything like that.
I remember, we used to-- we always walked-- even in the coldest weather, we had shorts on, and we had stockings right up to our-- you know, over our knees.
And well, maybe the winters weren't so h-- so cold in Hungary, you know?
So that what it was.
What grade did you go to in this public school?
I think I went as far as-- it went as far as the second grade, second or third grade.
I'm not exactly sure.
I wasn't afraid of the-- I wasn't aware of the grades.
And I remember already learning writing.
Reading, I already learned when I was very young.
My, my mother had a helper, you know, and she used to teach me reading.
And I read very early.
I read when I was three.
Reading in Hungarian, yes.
Yes.
Occasionally I heard my parents speak Yiddish, but not much.
Sometimes I spoke German.
And sometimes I spoke Czechoslovakian because Totkomlos is near the Czechoslovak border.
A lot of Toath was spoken.
T-O-A-T-H was spoken there.
Yes.
Oh yeah.
Yeah, of course.
I remember the Shabbat dinners.
And I remember a lot of candles.
We used to light a candle, candle holders-- what do you call them?
Candelabras.
OK.
We had about four or five candles for each uncle that passed away or each relative that we wanted to think about.
So they lit the candles.
Then we had the nice challah, you know, the ceremonial bread.
And it was covered with a beautiful cover, embroidered beautifully.
The Friday night dinners at our place was always gorgeous.
I always loved it.
I could taste the soup.
It was beautiful.
Golden soup, with little square noodles, you know?
Then we had boiled chicken.
Then we had boiled carp.
And then-- Do you remember any anti-Semitism in the town?
Yes.
The anti-Semitism came in the form of the cross arrows.
You know, they used to have cross arrows in Hungary.
They used to draw a cross.
And on the top of each cross, there was an arrow.
And they were called nyilasok, the, the arrow shooters.
And they were the-- they were the real Nazis.
And they used to harass the Jews, you know, the shopkeepers.
They used to cause trouble in the town all the time.
And we used to see their inscription in the walls all the time.
In other words-- and they were always desecrating the, the temple walls, you know, the shul walls.
No.
I don't remember any incidences.
Well, I mean, I saw the signs on the walls that they painted.
And I heard stories all the time, what they were.
Then we saw them occasionally when we went past the bars, we saw them, you know, talking and shouting at Jews, you know-- dirty Jews, stinking Jews, and stuff like that.
Well, the first sign of danger was when I remember one afternoon my mother-- I was in the store.
And my mother came, came into the store, and she was crying.
And you don't what to do.
I'd never seen my mother cry, you know, because usually she never showed herself crying to me.
It was a sign of weakness.
And I was-- didn't know what to do.
And I asked, mother, why are you crying?
And she said, we have to leave.
I said, oh, great.
We'll leave because every time-- every time my mother got-- my mother got homesick for her parents, we used to go to Szarvas.
Szarvas was north of us, oh, about 80 kilometers or 100 kilometers north of us.
And we figured we were going to go to Szarvas.
I said, oh, we're going.
Oh, great.
Great.
But then I started realizing weren't-- we weren't going anywhere to a good place.
But when we started to-- she got some sheets, you know, from the cupboard and she put as much coats and outfits, you know, into the sheets as possible and clothes.
And that was strange.
And she bundled up the sheets, and she just made a huge bundle of the sheets.
And she says, we have to go.
I said, where are we going?
There's no train.
We have to get the tickets to the train.
She says, no.
We are going.
We're going to the ghetto.
I know to a lot of people ghetto doesn't mean such a horrible thing, because ghettos, Jews used to live in ghettos 100 years ago, 200 years ago.
Cities used to have places where there were special sections of the city were set aside for Jews, and they were called ghettos.
But here in Hungary, ghetto me-- meant at this time was that they are taking us away, and it's going to be-- and it's going to be terrible.
I mean, anyway, the way she said it, we are going to the ghetto, we are going away, that was the first sign I knew that anti-Semitism has touched us, and that we have leave.
I said, what about our house?
I mean, are we leaving everything?
What about our maid, you know, the lady who took care of me, and I was very fond of.
She says, no, nobody.
We don't come.
We just-- we have to go and we have to walk.
So we walked into this big, big garage-like structure in the city.
Who was we?
My mother and I.
My father at this time was on the Russian front.
He was in the Hungarian army.
He was in the Hungarian soldiers.
But the Jews in Hungary who were soldiering, they outfitted them with the regular, you know, soldier outfits.
But they didn't fight.
They were-- they were in the work detail.
They were digging traps for tanks.
They were making-- you know, they were pulling-- what do you call those-- gates, you know, for tanks, you know, to get entangled in.
You know, those barbed wire fences and so on.
In other words, he was in work detail.
He left already about seven months before we went to the ghetto.
He, he was taken away to the front, to the Russian front.
It was trouble.
And the first-- my remembrance when my father was still there, my mother was still there, when in the store I remember seeing 1942.
I was-- I could read 1942, and I remember the calendar.
And I remember it was Hanukkah, because my father was making a dreidel, you know, out of lead.
He, he was very skillful.
He made it with wood, and he took four pieces of wood.
He put it together.
He started pouring the lead.
I remem-- so I remember then.
And shortly after that, he left into the soldiers.
And then after that, they took us to the ghetto.
And we all went to the ghetto.
No.
I-- every-- no.
We did not go into hiding or anything like that.
We were-- we had family.
We had family in Szarvas.
We-- my, my mother every four or five weeks took me to Szarvas, and I was with my grandparents.
It was a great time, you know?
I had my own little bed.
My grandparents treated me royally.
I went to cheder, where I wasn't responsible for any, any exams because I was there only for a short time.
So this was important for me.
This was a great thing for me, you know, my time is Szarvas, you know.
And I remember we used to be dressed in our beautiful hats with black kaftans.
I had my white socks on and so on.
In Szarvas actually is the first time where-- never mind.
I don't want to tell you that story.
But anyway, so she sent me to Szarvas all the time with my grandparents, with whom I always had a very nice time.
Came back and I had absolutely no notion that she was ever going to hide anywhere or even--
No.
I wasn't aware of-- well, occasionally on the newspaper I read.
I could read at this time that the English were making a move here, and the Germans were bombing this.
And that there was a big push going on in a certain part of the world.
I mean, I read some of the war news.
But to meet it was all-- meant nothing, really.
It just meant nothing to me.
I was-- I looked at everything, you know-- I mean, you have to realize I looked at everything with the eyes of a six-year-old or a seven-year-old.

Even when I was in the concentration camp and so on, everything was through the eyes of a child who was eight who goes through these crises who remembers everything but who didn't-- who wasn't afraid because he was with his mother.
If I wouldn't have had my mother, it could have been a different, very traumatic thing for me.
But I was lucky that I always had my mother with me.
And even when I, I-- with my knowledge, with cerebral-- cerebrally, I knew that we will never survive.
It still didn't mean too much to me because I was still with my mother.
And my mother had such a hold over me, and she created this aura of safety.
There may have been something which may have been important to me, but I, I must tell you the truth.
I don't remember.
And I can't make anything up because it wouldn't be nice.
But I don't rem-- that part, I don't remember.
I remember that we went and we had a hard time settling because the poorer families naturally got the poorer spots, you know, in the rooms that were available.
And the better heeled families always got the better spots, you know?
So I remember we had a hard time settling.
And there, there were about 110, 118 families, you know, in the-- in Totkomlos at that time.
How did you get to the ghetto?
We walked.
It was in the middle of the town.
We, we had a small town, remember.
It was a small town, like if you'd go to-- what could you call a small town just outside of Toronto?
Not even Belleville.
I mean, Belleville is really a big town.
I'm talking about--
So it was a short walk to the ghetto.
It was a short walk to the ghetto.
And everybody was watching us.
It was embarrassing because people were watching us, people who knew us.
When we arrived, they assigned us rooms.
And there was a big meeting.
They pulled us all into this middle of this big opening.
And the gendarme, the rendorseg, as it was called in Hungarian, they told us how to behave, not to escape because if we did anything like that, we would be shot.
So that was the first time I heard that our lives-- we were going to be shot.
Sounded like a, a comic tale, like a something you see in a movie, you know?
You saw people being shot in movie.
And I knew that it was make-believe.
But actually, we were going to be shot.
And I could feel my mother you know, stiffening you know, when she heard this.
And she started crying and the tears.
And, and I was concerned, I must say.
Some of the-- well, well, we couldn't leave the ghetto.
We needed permission to see a doctor or to see a dentist.
Anybody caught outside the ghetto was supposed to be shot.
But nobody was shot.
I mean, it was a little village.
Pardon?
No.
No.
We just didn't try to leave.
We-- I don't know.
There may have been some escapees.
I don't know.
I heard something, but, but I don't remember if anybody wanted to escape.
We heard stories that they are going to take us to, to a place where we're going to work.
We're going to be in a work detail.
We were going to get paid.
We're going to be like a displaced workforce.
And then after the war, we are going to be brought back.
I mean, we heard you know, the Hitler harangues, you know, how he was talking against the Jews and so on.
But we-- but we never-- we never thought that they are going to take it to that length.
It was just-- it was just incroyable.
I mean, it was impossible to think such things.
And we were-- [SIGHS] and we, we thought that we were good Hungarians, that we were-- we loved Hungary and so on, and that our religion was Jewish, but so, you know, you had your religion.
So we, we considered ourselves good people, you know, like good Hungarians.
And then--
Well, for us kids, well, we used to take sunbathing, tanning.
We use to tan.
I used to get such burns.
I remember I had a burn of my life there once.
And then-- and then we have cheder.
They, they kept us teaching.
Whatever a Jew does, the kid has to be educated.
So no matter what, what kind of horrible situation we were in, they always made it a little bit worse for the kids, you know?
I mean, we had to study [LAUGHS] while we were incarcerated.
So we were studying and--
I don't remember.
That, I don't remember.
Remember, I was a kid.
I just thought, well, my mother--
Yeah.
There was enough food.
That's right.
Yeah.
Nothing smashing or anything like that.
I remember an old lady cooking outside a chicken.
It was boiled.
It was stuffed with something very good.
And I remember just watching it for a half hour until she was pouring the juice over the-- I remember it as I'm sitting here, watching it, and I, I could've eaten.
I mean, we didn't have chicken, you know, at that time already.
We were not aware where the food was coming from.
I'm sure we paid for it because people still had money.
And they let us keep our money and so on.
They didn't take anybody's fillings or any teeth or any stories like that.
They, they said that they are not going to take our houses or anything like that.
In other words, they are not going to-- they're going to keep our houses for us.
But we knew better.
I mean, my mother beforehand-- I mean, I didn't know about this.
But my mother has, has dug some holes and put some silverware in, you know, that-- you know, some of the family heirlooms and so on.
And she did-- some of the more important items, she took down into the well, loosened a couple of bricks, and put the-- put the jewelry or trinkets behind the brick.
She put that away.
But I don't remember it.
But I heard about it after we came back from the concentration camp.
Totkomlos, it was a nice place, I remember.
We had-- we had the equivalent of 7-Eleven that we have here, except over there, it was like-- it was like a pub.
You went in there.
You could have a sandwich.
And then they used to make you spritzers.
A big thing was a spritzer.
We used to have sweet wine with club soda together.
And that's what we used to have.
And they used to sell herrings at the same time.
It was very interesting.
I used to love-- my father used to take me in for spritzers and herrings and some bread.
I have some nice remembrances.
Your father took you in--
To the pubs.
To the pubs.
This was before the ghetto.
Before he left.
Yes, before '42 even, you know.
So these pubs were no longer there once you were in a ghetto.
When in the ghetto, no.
We couldn't go to a pub.
We couldn't go anywhere.
We couldn't go to a movie.
We couldn't do anything.
We were just waiting to be picked up and taken away.
I'm not sure.
I think-- it feels like it was four, five months, could've been even six months, but maybe not that long.
But you know, for a child, everything is long.
I mean, even a day.
I mean, we couldn't wait until a day finished.
And then we used to have prayers, you know, every night.
That took time.
Prayers twice in the afternoon.
Prayers at night.
Prayers on Saturday and so on.
And we were just waiting.
But I'm not exactly sure about the time.
Could have been four months, maybe less or maybe more.
I'm not sure.
And you were waiting to be taken to a place where you would be going to work.
That's right.
Yeah.
We always heard about that.
Yes, it did happen-- did happen about four months afterwards.
They told us.
They all brought this into the big room.
And they made us-- they gave us a couple of threats-- what would happen if we leave, they're going to shoot us.
And then they told us that they're going to take us away in a train, and that we have to be ready.
And we weren't sure when they were going to take us.
They told us, we'll just come.
We'll just come, and then we'll pick you up.
So, so what happened, one night around 2:00-- I think it was 2:00 in the morning or 1:30 in the morning-- they told us all to get up.
And they told us to get moving.
So we picked up all our belongings.
Naturally, my mother fitted me out with two winter coats, you know, put two winter coats on me, you know, because she figured that who knows what may happen?
I mean, she had some forebodings, and people were talking, you know, so we thought, you know, it may not come to a good end.
And so then she gathered her things together and put everything in those big valises, and we tied them up with cords and stuff.
And we just got moving.
And it was just a long line going through the village.
And it was night.
There were no lights.
Yeah. the entire ghetto was moved to the rail station.
And the whole town was blacked out, you know, because of the planes and the air raids and so on.
And but we could feel the eyes of the-- of the Christians, you know, watching us from behind darkened windows.
And we are moving, and some of them rapped on the window a little bit because we were close.
You know, we were just that yay far away from-- because it's small streets in a village.
We don't know, but I think some of them were happy.
And some of them, you know, they couldn't do anything because if they would have done something, they would've-- they would've-- the same fate would have befallen them as had befallen us.
So but most of them were probably happy.
And they were already figuring out what to do, you know, how to take away, you know, our possessions and how to-- how to see what they can do.
But it's just the feeling we had was from behind those darkened windows that we could feel their eyes, you know, watching us as we were going.
It was a long trek.
It was cold, I remember.
And I remember the cattle cars.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember it was-- it was weird because I expected regular cars that you sit in, you know, with third class, second class, first class.
But this was cattle cars.
And I remember, in my eyes-- I mean, I asked my mother, why?
What's happening?
And, and she wasn't-- she was also shocked.
And I just-- Did you know that these were the cars that you were going to be loaded onto?
No, we didn't know.
We didn't know.
And they just started loading us up.

And they just pushed us in, you know, like in a-- like, you know, in one of those-- you know, you see those Japanese subway, you know, scenes, you know, where the people are crowding in, and these-- and these people, these officials are pushing them into it.
It was the same thing with us.
It was just unbelievable.
I don't know.
Maybe 50, maybe 60.
A lot.
And there was no place to sit, no place to do this or that.
It wasn't so bad, because it's-- you know, this trip wasn't so long.
It went just to Szeged, you know, to this distribution camp.
Of course, we didn't know where we were going to go.
But thank god, it didn't go too long.
You know it maybe went maybe about 12 hours or 16 hours or who knows?
I don't know.
There was no water, no nothing.
It was our first taste of terror, of--
--of being uncomfortable.

Well, because, you know, when they press you into this cattle car, all of you, with the sick and with the well, with the healthy, with the old, with the young, with the no place to sit, I mean, you become-- you started becoming animals because you couldn't give your seat to anybody.
There was no seats.
You couldn't make anybody comfortable who was uncomfortable beside you.
People who had to go to the washroom, they just had to go to the washroom.
They did it in the cattle car.
And naturally, everything started smelling, because they couldn't remove their clothes, so the clothes got stained and the-- and the human excrement.
You know, this-- then, it started, this whole, this horrible, this foreboding, the, the game, the dance, you know?
The dance started.
You know, this, the macabre, you know, it just started.
And that's how it was.
